stem: Which finding is most consistent with the BNP pattern?

tabs.case_study: Dyspnea, orthopnea, bilateral crackles.
tabs.labs: BNP 3133 pg/mL (↑) ; Na 129 mmol/L ; Cr 0.9 mg/dL

choices: Volume overload/ventricular stretch consistent with heart failure exacerbation.
Primary asthma exacerbation without cardiac contribution.
Early DKA as the main driver.
answer: Volume overload/ventricular stretch consistent with heart failure exacerbation.
rationale: Markedly elevated BNP reflects myocardial stretch from increased filling pressures, supporting decompensated heart failure in the right clinical context.

Septic shock with primary metabolic alkalosis

Encourage breathing into paper bag only
Give sodium bicarbonate as first-line without addressing ventilation
Send home; reassure

No ABG change regardless of ventilation
Hypoxemia resolves by withholding oxygen

stem: Complete the NGN bowtie using the case information in the tabs.

tabs.labs: Na 121 (↓), Serum Osm 258 (↓), Urine Osm 520 (↑), Urine Na 62 (↑); no edema; normal BUN/Cr

middle_options: Fluid restriction and treat cause per protocol; monitor neuro status
Give hypotonic fluids rapidly
Administer desmopressin immediately
Encourage free water intake without limits
right_options: Serum sodium rises gradually as free water excretion improves
Serum sodium drops further due to more free water retention
Urine becomes more dilute without any change
Seizure risk disappears instantly regardless of correction rate

answer.middle: Fluid restriction and treat cause per protocol; monitor neuro status
answer.right: Serum sodium rises gradually as free water excretion improves

rationale: Inappropriately concentrated urine in hypotonic hyponatremia without edema suggests SIADH; controlled correction reduces neurologic risk.

stem: Which clinical risk is most consistent with this lab pattern in cirrhosis?

tabs.case_study: Confusion and asterixis after GI bleed/constipation.
tabs.labs: Ammonia 98 µmol/L (↑); INR 2.0 (↑)

choices: Hepatic encephalopathy risk requiring neuro monitoring and escalation.
Primary bacterial meningitis confirmed by ammonia.
answer: Hepatic encephalopathy risk requiring neuro monitoring and escalation.
rationale: Elevated ammonia in liver disease can contribute to encephalopathy, especially with impaired clearance/synthetic dysfunction.

stem: Which physiologic explanation best fits elevated BUN in an upper GI bleed?

tabs.case_study: Coffee-ground emesis and dizziness.
tabs.labs: Hgb 7.2 g/dL (↓); BUN 57 mg/dL (↑); Stool guaiac positive

choices: Digested blood proteins increase urea production/absorption, raising BUN disproportionately.
BUN rises only with kidney stones.
Upper GI bleed lowers BUN by protein loss.
BUN is unrelated to GI bleeding.
answer: Digested blood proteins increase urea production/absorption, raising BUN disproportionately.
rationale: Upper GI bleeding increases absorbed protein load (digested hemoglobin) → increased urea formation → BUN rises.

choice_rationales.Digested blood proteins increase urea pr: Correct—this finding/action best matches the lab pattern and physiology in the stem.
Incorrect—plausible but does not best fit the lab pattern, or it is unsafe / not the priority.
choice_rationales.Upper GI bleed lowers BUN by protein los: Incorrect—plausible but does not best fit the lab pattern, or it is unsafe / not the priority.
Incorrect—plausible but does not best fit the lab pattern, or it is unsafe / not the priority.

answer.right: Pain and inflammatory markers improve as pancreatic inflammation resolves; complications are monitored early

rationale: Very high lipase with classic pain supports pancreatitis; early priorities are supportive care and monitoring for systemic complications.

stem: Which complication should the nurse prioritize monitoring for with this calcium pattern?

stem: Which interpretation is most accurate?

tabs.case_study: Ataxia and nystagmus noted.
tabs.labs: Phenytoin total 27.8 µg/mL (↑); Albumin 2.6 g/dL (↓)

choices: Risk for phenytoin toxicity is high—low albumin increases free (active) fraction; escalate symptoms monitoring.
Level is therapeutic because albumin is low.
Low albumin prevents phenytoin from acting.
Phenytoin level is unrelated to neuro findings.
answer: Risk for phenytoin toxicity is high—low albumin increases free (active) fraction; escalate symptoms monitoring.
rationale: Low albumin increases free phenytoin; total level may underestimate toxicity risk—clinical signs (nystagmus, ataxia) matter.

stem: Which statement best explains the pulse oximetry finding?

tabs.case_study: Headache after exposure to heater fumes.
tabs.labs: Carboxyhemoglobin 22% (↑); PaO2 normal; SpO2 reads 98%

choices: Pulse oximetry can appear normal in CO poisoning because it cannot distinguish oxyhemoglobin from carboxyhemoglobin.
CO poisoning always lowers PaO2, so SpO2 must be low.
SpO2 measures COHb directly, so it is accurate here.
CO increases oxygen content, so saturation rises.
answer: Pulse oximetry can appear normal in CO poisoning because it cannot distinguish oxyhemoglobin from carboxyhemoglobin.
rationale: Standard pulse oximeters cannot reliably detect dyshemoglobins; COHb can cause falsely normal SpO2.

stem: Which physiologic explanation best links alkalemia to worsening confusion in liver disease?

tabs.case_study: Cirrhosis with recent vomiting/diuretics.
tabs.labs: Ammonia 205 µmol/L (↑); pH 7.55 (alkalemic)

choices: Alkalemia increases NH3 fraction, which crosses BBB more readily, worsening encephalopathy.
Alkalemia converts NH3 to NH4+ making CNS entry easier.
pH has no effect on ammonia species.
Confusion is unrelated to ammonia in liver failure.
answer: Alkalemia increases NH3 fraction, which crosses BBB more readily, worsening encephalopathy.
rationale: Higher pH favors NH3 (nonionized), which crosses BBB more readily than NH4+.

choice_rationales.Alkalemia increases NH3 fraction, which: Correct—this finding/action best matches the lab pattern and physiology in the stem.

stem: Which statement best explains the pulse oximetry finding?

tabs.case_study: Headache after exposure to heater fumes.
tabs.labs: Carboxyhemoglobin 22% (↑); PaO2 normal; SpO2 reads 98%

choices: Pulse oximetry can appear normal in CO poisoning because it cannot distinguish oxyhemoglobin from carboxyhemoglobin.
CO poisoning always lowers PaO2, so SpO2 must be low.
SpO2 measures COHb directly, so it is accurate here.
CO increases oxygen content, so saturation rises.
answer: Pulse oximetry can appear normal in CO poisoning because it cannot distinguish oxyhemoglobin from carboxyhemoglobin.
rationale: Standard pulse oximeters cannot reliably detect dyshemoglobins; COHb can cause falsely normal SpO2.

stem: Which interpretation is most accurate?

tabs.case_study: Ataxia and nystagmus noted.
tabs.labs: Phenytoin total 37.5 µg/mL (↑); Albumin 2.8 g/dL (↓)

choices: Risk for phenytoin toxicity is high—low albumin increases free (active) fraction; escalate symptoms monitoring.
Level is therapeutic because albumin is low.
Low albumin prevents phenytoin from acting.
Phenytoin level is unrelated to neuro findings.
answer: Risk for phenytoin toxicity is high—low albumin increases free (active) fraction; escalate symptoms monitoring.
rationale: Low albumin increases free phenytoin; total level may underestimate toxicity risk—clinical signs (nystagmus, ataxia) matter.

stem: Complete the NGN bowtie using the case information in the tabs.

tabs.labs: Na 114 (↓), Serum Osm 244 (↓), Seizure; urine osmolality high

left_options: Severe hypotonic hyponatremia causing cerebral edema
Hypoglycemia as sole cause despite normal glucose
middle_options: Seizure safety + urgent hyponatremia escalation pathway; controlled correction per protocol
Give large free-water bolus
Rapidly correct sodium to normal immediately without monitoring
No action; observe
right_options: Seizure risk decreases as sodium is corrected at a safe rate and brain swelling improves
Risk increases as sodium rises slowly
No relationship between sodium and seizures
Urine becomes dilute regardless of therapy

answer.left: Severe hypotonic hyponatremia causing cerebral edema
answer.middle: Seizure safety + urgent hyponatremia escalation pathway; controlled correction per protocol
answer.right: Seizure risk decreases as sodium is corrected at a safe rate and brain swelling improves

rationale: Severe hypotonic hyponatremia can cause cerebral edema and seizures; priority is seizure safety and controlled correction to prevent osmotic demyelination.

stem: Complete the NGN bowtie using the case information in the tabs.

tabs.labs: CK 42,000 (↑↑), K 6.6 (↑), Cr 3.1 (↑); urine dip blood+ with few RBCs

middle_options: Aggressive IV fluids per protocol; monitor/treat hyperkalemia; renal monitoring
Give potassium supplementation
Restrict fluids to prevent edema without direction
Delay care until CK normalizes
right_options: AKI and dysrhythmia risk decrease as myoglobin load and potassium are managed
Hyperkalemia resolves only by giving more K
Myoglobin cannot affect kidneys
CK normalizes immediately

No action is needed because antibody screen is negative.

tabs.labs: Chronic ulcer: ESR 116 (↑); CRP 138 (↑); probe-to-bone positive

choices: High suspicion for osteomyelitis—requires imaging/culture pathway and escalation.
No infection risk because fever absent.
answer: High suspicion for osteomyelitis—requires imaging/culture pathway and escalation.
rationale: In a chronic ulcer probing to bone, markedly elevated ESR/CRP supports osteomyelitis concern even without high fever.

stem: Which explanation best links this lab to impaired wound healing?

tabs.case_study: Nonhealing surgical wound, poor intake.
tabs.labs: Prealbumin 9.6 mg/dL (↓); Wound dehiscence risk increased

choices: Protein-calorie malnutrition reduces substrate for collagen synthesis and immune function.
answer: Protein-calorie malnutrition reduces substrate for collagen synthesis and immune function.
rationale: Low prealbumin suggests inadequate protein stores/acute malnutrition, which impairs collagen formation and immune response.

stem: Which physiologic mechanism best explains edema in this context?

tabs.case_study: Pressure injury risk with swelling.
tabs.labs: Albumin 1.9 g/dL (↓); generalized edema; chronic wound

choices: Low oncotic pressure from hypoalbuminemia promotes fluid shift into interstitium.
High oncotic pressure pulls fluid into tissues.
Albumin increases capillary hydrostatic pressure directly.
Edema is unrelated to plasma proteins.
answer: Low oncotic pressure from hypoalbuminemia promotes fluid shift into interstitium.
rationale: Albumin is a major oncotic determinant; low albumin reduces plasma oncotic pressure, favoring interstitial edema.

stem: Which interpretation best fits this early burn lab pattern?

tabs.case_study: Tachycardic, cool extremities.
tabs.labs: Burn 35% TBSA: Hct 56.0% (↑); Na 129 (↓); Lactate 3.7 (↑)

choices: Hemoconcentration and hypoperfusion from capillary leak (burn shock physiology).
answer: Hemoconcentration and hypoperfusion from capillary leak (burn shock physiology).
rationale: Early burns cause plasma loss into interstitium, raising hematocrit and lactate; sodium may fall with shifts/resuscitation.

stem: Which interpretation best matches this lab pattern in a febrile hypotensive patient with a wound infection?

tabs.case_study: Necrotic wound with purulent drainage.
tabs.labs: Lactate 7.1 mmol/L (↑); WBC 23 (↑); Procalcitonin 11.9 (↑)

choices: Sepsis with tissue hypoperfusion—activate sepsis bundle per protocol.
Normal perfusion because WBC is high.
answer: Sepsis with tissue hypoperfusion—activate sepsis bundle per protocol.
rationale: Elevated lactate reflects hypoperfusion; high procalcitonin supports bacterial infection severity requiring urgent sepsis management.

stem: Complete the NGN bowtie using the case information in the tabs.

tabs.labs: CRP 290 (↑↑), WBC 28 (↑), Na 126 (↓), Lactate 7.8 (↑); pain out of proportion; rapidly spreading erythema

middle_options: Immediate escalation for surgical evaluation + sepsis pathway per protocol
Apply topical moisturizer and discharge
Delay evaluation until culture results return
Give diuretic only
right_options: Improved survival and limb preservation with rapid source control and resuscitation
No change because surgery never helps
Lactate rises as infection resolves
Pain resolves without intervention

answer.middle: Immediate escalation for surgical evaluation + sepsis pathway per protocol
answer.right: Improved survival and limb preservation with rapid source control and resuscitation

rationale: The lab pattern plus disproportionate pain suggests necrotizing infection with hypoperfusion; rapid surgical evaluation and resuscitation are time-critical.
